1: māśśāʾ (H4853) — "oracle/burden" — from nāśāʾ (to lift/carry); used for prophetic pronouncements against foreign nations (cf. Isaiah 13:1; Isaiah 17:1; Isaiah 19:1; Isaiah 21:1; Isaiah 22:1; Isaiah 23:1). The word suggests both a pronouncement and a heavy load. layl (night) twice — "in a single night... in a single night" — emphasizes the suddenness and totality of Moab's fall. šāddad (H7703) — devastated/destroyed.
2: bāmôt (H1116) — high places, the hilltop shrines characteristic of Moabite (and Canaanite) worship. Dibon, Nebo, Medeba — specific Moabite cities east of the Dead Sea, each with its own sacred identity. yillîl (H3213) — to wail; the verb is onomatopoeic, suggesting the sound of mourning. The shaving of beard (kol-rōʾš qārēḥâ) and cutting of hair (kol-zāqān gĕruʿâ) are ancient mourning practices (cf. Isaiah 22:12).
3: The city squares (rĕḥōbôt) and rooftops are where public lamentation takes place — weeping is communal and visible. The word yārēd (going down) may describe both the procession to the valley and the descent into sorrow. yĕlîlâ — the howling/wailing sound; a vivid auditory image of a city in communal grief.
4: The cities of Moab cry out — Heshbon and Elealeh (ṣāʿăqû, raised their voice) — their voices reach even to Jahaz (nišmaʿ qôlām, their voice is heard). The Moabite warriors (ḥalûṣê Môʾāḇ, the armed men of Moab) themselves tremble (rāʿăšâ nap̄šô, his soul quivers/trembles). The prophet is describing a collapse of military nerve alongside civic grief.
5: Isaiah's own lament: libbî lĕMôʾāḇ yizʿāq — "my heart cries out for Moab." This is one of the most striking features of Isaiah's oracles against foreign nations — the prophet does not gloat but mourns. The fugitives flee to Zoar (cf. Genesis 19:22, where Lot fled to Zoar) and to Eglath-shelishiyah. The road to Lûḥît and Ḥôrônayim — specific Moabite escape routes — suggests detailed geographical knowledge.
6: mê Nimrîm — the "waters of Nimrim"; unidentified but probably a fertile region now deserted. šāʾôt (desolate) and yābēš (dried up) — the drying of water sources signals ecological as well as social collapse. dešeʾ yārāq ʾênennu — "the green grass is no more" — the loss of vegetation in an arid region is catastrophic.
7: yitrāh — the "surplus" or "accumulated wealth"; what they have saved (pĕqudātô, their stored things). The refugees carry whatever they can over the wādî haʿărābîm (the Brook of the Willows) — probably the Wadi el-Hesa (Zered). The image is of a complete evacuation.
8: The weeping pervades the entire border of Moab — sāḇab (surrounds/goes all around) — from Eglaim to Beer-elim. Every named location emphasizes that no part of Moab escapes grief. This is a total national lamentation, not a local disaster.
9: mê Dîmôn — "the waters of Dimon"; possibly a pun on dām (blood), since māleʾ dāmîm (full of blood) follows. The wordplay intensifies the horror. ʾaryēh (lion) as the destroyer who pursues the survivors — a standard image for a conquering enemy (cf. Amos 3:12). The survivors of Moab face a final threat even after the initial destruction.
